title: Memory Arenas
use_for: designing pass-local or phase-local temporary memory
replacing repeated temporary allocations with reset-at-boundary arenas
separating scratch working memory from long-lived ownership
making temporary memory cost predictable in staged execution
containing transient allocations inside explicit lifecycle boundaries
do_not_use_for: pooling stable reusable objects with individual lifetimes
UI architecture as a primary owner
rules: use arenas for grouped temporary lifetime, not for arbitrary shared ownership
reset arenas at explicit lifecycle boundaries
keep arena ownership single and inspectable
separate scratch memory from retained products and long-lived state
cap or observe retained peak capacity
procedure: Identify the pass or phase with repeated temporary memory churn.
Define the arena owner and reset boundary.
Move short-lived scratch allocations into linear or grouped arena use.
Keep long-lived outputs outside the arena.
Measure allocation churn, retained peak size, and reset cost.

primary_when: the request is specifically about scratch memory, frame arenas, pass-local temporary memory, or grouped reset-at-boundary allocation
the request is about replacing many short-lived temporary allocations with arena-style lifetime control
must_not_own_when: the main issue is long-lived object pooling, generic ownership policy, or cache locality without grouped temporary lifetime

solves: how to give temporary working memory one explicit owner and reset point
how to reduce churn from many short-lived allocations inside one phase
how to keep scratch memory separate from retained outputs
does_not_solve: general memory policy across all lifetimes
stable object reuse with individual return semantics

core_principles: Arenas are for grouped temporary lifetime, not for hiding broad ownership ambiguity.
Reset boundaries should align with real runtime phases or passes.
Scratch memory and retained outputs should never blur into one storage class.
constraints: Arena lifetime must be short, explicit, and owned by one runtime boundary.
Anything that survives the reset boundary must leave the arena deliberately.
Peak arena growth must be observable so one burst does not become permanent memory bloat.
decision_rules: Use memory-arenas as the owner when many small temporary allocations belong to one pass or phase and can die together.
If temporary memory spans multiple phases unpredictably, do not force it into one arena.
If responsiveness matters, attach work-budgeting so scratch growth and reset behavior stay compatible with the phase budget.
If the memory problem is really poor staging or layout, attach data-oriented-design instead of solving everything with arenas.

preferred_patterns: phase-local scratch arenas with explicit reset
arena outputs copied or promoted explicitly when they must survive
arena ownership tied to one system, stage, or pass
observable peak capacity and reset behavior
avoid_patterns: shared global scratch arenas across unrelated systems
using arenas to hide uncertain ownership
keeping peak-sized arenas forever with no capacity discipline

recipes.name: Replace pass-local churn with a scratch arena
recipes.when: Use when one pass repeatedly allocates temporary working memory that dies at the same boundary.
steps: Name the pass boundary and arena owner.
Move grouped temporary allocations into the arena.
Ensure outputs that must survive are promoted out explicitly.
Reset at the pass boundary and measure retained peak capacity.

pitfalls: Letting data escape an arena reset boundary.
Using one shared arena for unrelated systems.
Applying arenas where individual object reuse or long-lived ownership is the real need.
review_checklist: Is the reset boundary real and deterministic?
Are long-lived outputs kept out of the arena?
Is peak arena growth observed and controlled?

references.title: Local Reference: Memory Arenas
references.interpretation: Use this first for grouped temporary lifetime, reset boundaries, and scratch-memory ownership.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for arena-style temporary memory.

references.title: Memory Management
references.interpretation: Use this to keep arena usage aligned with the broader lifetime and ownership model.
references.why: Use to connect arena decisions to the domain's general memory discipline.
